turns.text: Hello Cherri, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s strange sitting here. Usually, at this time of day, I’m in the NICU, checking saturation levels and adjusting leads. I’m used to being the one with the answers, the one people look to when things get scary. But now? I’m just Cherri, sitting in my living room in Pembroke, and the quiet is… deafening.

I’m grateful she’s healthy—my pregnancy was completely textbook, thank goodness—but I think being a nurse has made this transition harder, not easier. I can't just be a mom. Every time she lets out a cry, my brain immediately cycles through a differential diagnosis. Is that a wheeze? Is her color okay? I find myself holding her and watching her chest rise and fall with this clinical, analytical intensity that I can’t seem to turn off. It’s exhausting.

The hardest part, honestly, is the appointments. My OB and the nurses see my chart, see my credentials, and they just shift into this professional shorthand. They assume I already know everything, so they skip the soft, reassuring things they say to other mothers. I’m sitting there, feeling incredibly raw and lonely as a single mom, and I’m just dying for someone to look at me—not as a colleague, but as a person—and tell me I’m doing a good job. I’m trying, but I’ve never felt more vulnerable.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I think, deep down, I expected my recovery to be just another "textbook" outcome. During my shifts in the NICU, I’ve seen women go through so much—emergency sections, complications, trauma—and because my own pregnancy was so clean, I think I foolishly convinced myself that my body would just bounce back. I treated my own health like another patient chart: low risk, expected standard healing timeline, no issues. I expected the fatigue, sure, but I didn't account for the way my professional brain would sabotage the healing process.

The reality? It wasn't the physical pain that tripped me up. My body healed fine, technically. But I couldn't rest. How do you recover when your mind is running an internal audit on your own vitals every time you sit down? I was so hyper-vigilant about my daughter’s breathing that I completely ignored my own needs. I stopped caring about how I felt physically because I was too busy scanning her for things that weren't even there. I think I expected "recovery" to be about physical repair, but the reality has been trying to figure out how to stop being a NICU nurse so I can actually be a mother. I’m physically fine, but the mental recovery feels like a long, lonely road.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I thought I had it all mapped out. Because I work in a hospital, I’d been around lactation consultants and I’d seen it all—or so I thought. I assumed it would be mechanical, you know? Just another physiological function to manage. I expected that because I knew the "how-to" from a technical standpoint, the actual bonding and the physical rhythm would just snap into place.

But the reality? It’s been nothing like that. Feeding her is the time I feel that "clinical brain" of mine the most intensely. When she’s at my breast, instead of just feeling the closeness, I find myself obsessively checking her suck-swallow-breathe pattern. If she pauses for a second, my heart rate spikes. I’m not just feeding her; I’m monitoring her. And doing that alone—without someone to hand the baby off to or to just tell me I’m doing okay—it’s incredibly isolating. 

I didn't expect the emotional weight of it. I thought it was just calories and nutrition, but it’s become this high-stakes, high-anxiety task. I’m constantly wondering if I’m missing some subtle sign that something is wrong. I just want to sit there and feed her without feeling like I’m on a twelve-hour shift. I want to feel like a mother, not a technician.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

Oh, absolutely. There was a massive gap. Because of my background, the nurses and lactation consultants operated on this assumption that I already "got it." They’d breeze into the room, give me a quick, professional nod, and ask if I had any technical questions. They’d use the shorthand—talking about let-down reflexes and latch positioning in such a clinical, detached way. It felt like they were talking to a coworker, not a woman who was overwhelmed, sleep-deprived, and doing this entirely by herself.

What I actually needed wasn't a technical breakdown or a medical explanation. I needed someone to sit with me and just be a human. I needed someone to look at me and say, "You’re doing a great job, and it’s okay to be scared." I needed that soft, simple encouragement that I’ve heard them give to other patients—the kind of validation that reminds you you’re a person, not just a set of vitals. 

Instead, their silence felt like an expectation for me to be perfect. By treating me like a colleague, they effectively robbed me of the chance to be a vulnerable new mom. It felt like I wasn't allowed to struggle because I’m "supposed to know better." It’s incredibly lonely to be held to a standard you didn’t ask for when you’re just trying to keep your head above water.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I was arrogant enough to believe that my medical background would act as a buffer. I’ve seen postpartum depression, anxiety, and the "baby blues" so many times on the ward that I thought I’d be able to recognize the symptoms in myself and just... manage it. I treated my own emotional health like a diagnostic task. I thought, *If I feel X, I’ll just do Y to fix it.* I really believed I could control my headspace the same way I control a patient’s oxygen settings.

The reality hit me like a brick wall. It’s not a checklist; it’s a constant, low-level hum of terror that I can’t shut off. It’s not just "baby blues"—it’s this relentless, hyper-vigilant state where I’m terrified that if I relax, I’ll miss something critical. I’m living in a constant state of "clinical burnout," even at home. I thought I’d be prepared for the exhaustion, but I wasn't prepared for the crushing, silent weight of doing this alone. I spend my days feeling like I’m failing because I can’t just *be* with her; I’m always *observing* her. I crave the ability to just hold her without feeling like I’m on duty. It’s lonely, and frankly, it’s been far more draining than any twelve-hour shift I’ve ever worked.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

That’s a heavy question, but it’s the exact one that hits home. There is a massive, suffocating gap between what I feel and what I’m “allowed” to feel. Because I wear that nurse label, there’s an unspoken rule—from my own family, from the doctors, and mostly from myself—that I should be the "unflappable one." When I see my family, they look at me like I’m the expert. They don’t see the terror; they see the person who knows what to do if the baby chokes or runs a fever. It’s like I’ve been denied the right to be a beginner.

Professionally, it’s even worse. My OB and the nurses treat my anxiety like a character flaw rather than a legitimate postpartum experience. They don’t say it, but their eyes ask, *“Why are you struggling? You know the physiology.”* It makes me feel like I have to keep my mouth shut, like I have to perform “competent motherhood” at all times. 

I’m not allowed to be scared, because I “know” the statistics. I’m not allowed to be overwhelmed, because I’ve “seen worse.” It’s a performative silence that’s killing me. I’m just a mother who is exhausted, but the world keeps treating me like a clinical resource, and I’m just so tired of being that person.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I think I expected that becoming a mother would be a graceful transition—a new chapter that would just sit comfortably alongside my career. I thought I’d be "Cherri the Nurse" and "Cherri the Mom," and that they’d just blend seamlessly. I had this vision of myself as the cool, collected professional who happened to have a baby at home. I thought my identity would expand to include motherhood, not be consumed by it.

But the shift has been jarring. Right now, I don't feel like a person at all—I feel like a peripheral device to her life. My identity hasn't just expanded; it feels like it’s been erased and replaced by this singular, terrifying job title: *The Monitor.* I look in the mirror and I see someone who looks like me, but she feels like a stranger. My entire sense of self is now tied to her respiratory rate and her weight gain. 

I don't know how to exist without being "on." Even when she’s sleeping, I’m listening for sounds, waiting for the monitor I’ve built into my own head to go off. I’ve lost the version of myself that could just be. I’m mourning the woman I was—the one who could leave the hospital and leave the work behind. Now, I’m never off the clock.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

It’s the simple ability to switch off that I miss the most, and that’s been the hardest part to let go of. Before, I could walk out of the hospital doors, change out of my scrubs, and leave the NICU behind. I had this boundary—a physical and mental border—between the high-stakes world of medical crises and my own quiet life in Pembroke. I felt secure in who I was outside of those four walls.

But now, that border has completely collapsed. My professional identity has essentially colonized my personal life. I thought I could hold onto my social self, but how do I go for coffee with a friend when every conversation feels like a distraction from "monitoring" the baby? I feel like I'm constantly analyzing the people around me, too—looking at their habits or the environment through that same cold, medical lens. 

It’s isolating. I feel like I’ve lost the *spontaneity* of who I was. I’m so afraid that if I relax, if I let myself just be "Cherri the friend" or "Cherri the woman," that something will slip through the cracks. It’s a terrifying way to live, but I don’t know how to turn the "nurse" part of my brain down so the "me" part can breathe again.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I think I expected those check-ups to be a sanctuary. I honestly thought that once I was on the "patient" side of the desk, I’d be treated with the same gentle, comprehensive care I’ve seen my own mentors provide. I expected to be asked how *I* was holding up—not just physically, but as a new, single mother navigating the biggest shift of her life. I expected them to check for my well-being the same way they checked for the baby’s.

In practice? It felt like a clinical hand-off. The appointments were brisk, efficient, and almost exclusively focused on the baby’s milestones. When I’d try to bring up how anxious I was feeling, or the sheer weight of being a solo parent, it was like the air went out of the room. They’d give me these vague nods, like, "You’re a nurse, you know the drill, you’re doing great." It was a dismissal disguised as a compliment.

They checked my stitches, checked her weight, and checked the boxes on their forms. I left every single time feeling like a ghost. I didn't get care; I got a conversation between peers about a patient. They never stopped to see that the "colleague" they were talking to was drowning.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

I remember specifically trying to bring up the anxiety—how I couldn't stop the internal scanning, how it felt like I was constantly on a shift that never ended. I didn't use the word "anxiety" at first, I think I was too proud, but I described the hyper-vigilance, the lack of sleep, and the feeling that I was constantly waiting for a code blue in my own living room.

I expected the doctor to pause, maybe pull up a chair, and really dig into that. Instead, she just kind of brushed it off with a smile and said something like, "Well, Cherri, you know how it is in the NICU. You’re just used to the environment, and it’s normal to be cautious." It was framed as if my medical expertise was actually *causing* my symptoms, rather than my expertise being a reason to *address* them. 

She treated it like a personality quirk of being a nurse, not a mental health concern worth intervening in. I felt so dismissed. I left feeling like I was being "difficult" or "overreacting" by asking for help. It’s infuriating, really. Because I have that background, they assume I can "self-manage" or "regulate," so they never offer the support they’d offer anyone else. It made me realize that being a nurse is actually a barrier to being a patient.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

Returning to work felt like stepping into a parallel dimension. I expected it to be a relief, honestly. I thought that by returning to the NICU, I would finally reclaim my identity as "Cherri the Nurse" and that having that professional structure back would somehow balance out the chaos of motherhood. I thought, *Okay, I’ll be back in my element, and I won’t have to be the one on call for my daughter twenty-four-seven.*

The reality was, and still is, completely jarring. Stepping onto the floor, I felt like a stranger in my own skin. When I’m adjusting the equipment for my tiny patients, I’m constantly hit with this visceral, gut-wrenching wave of guilt. I’m doing for other people’s babies exactly what I’m terrified to do for mine. 

Physically, my body feels like it’s in two places at once. I’m working my twelve-hour shifts, but every single alarm that goes off in the unit makes my heart jump into my throat, not just for the patient, but because it triggers a fresh wave of panic about my own baby back home. I didn’t realize how much harder it would be to leave her, knowing exactly what can go wrong in a hospital—and what can go wrong when you're just at home in a crib. It’s not a relief; it’s just double the vigilance.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

It’s a bit of a difficult topic for me, truthfully. I’m a single mother, so I didn’t have a partner to navigate this with, but that’s exactly where the expectation and reality clash the hardest. 

Before she arrived, I think I built up this mental image of a "team"—a hypothetical partner who would take the night shift, someone I could hand the baby to when my clinical brain started spiraling, someone who would just *see* me. I expected that even if things got tough, there would be a soft landing waiting for me at home. I thought I wouldn't have to bear the weight of all these decisions and all this worry alone.

The reality of being a solo parent is just... complete, total responsibility. There is no one to rotate with. When I’m at home, I’m the only pair of eyes on her. I’m the only one who hears the cry, the only one who assesses the skin tone, the only one who carries the anxiety. I realized that the loneliness isn’t just about having someone to talk to; it’s about the lack of a witness. I’m doing all of this, and nobody sees the quiet, terrified work that goes into just getting through the day. It’s just me, the crib, and the silence.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

Since I’m a single mother, I suppose my "partnership" isn't with another person, but with the version of myself I thought I’d be. The biggest surprise, and the one that still stings, is the realization that I am truly the only one in the room. 

I expected that I’d be able to "tag out"—that I could mentally hand the baton of responsibility to someone else so I could sleep, or eat, or just stare at a wall without my heart rate spiking. But when you’re doing this alone, there is no "off" switch. I am the sole diagnostic tool, the sole caregiver, and the sole witness to her life. 

There’s this profound shock in realizing that I don’t have a buffer. When I get that gut-level, panicked feeling that something might be wrong with her, I have to be the one to talk myself down. I have to be the one to validate my own intuition. I expected that I’d be able to lean on a partner to tell me, "You’re doing a great job, take a breath, she’s fine." Instead, I have to be the one to look in the mirror and say that to myself, and honestly? It’s hard to believe yourself when your brain is hardwired for the worst-case scenario. That level of isolation—where you are both the alarm and the responder—is something I never truly grasped until I was living it.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected maternity care to be a bridge. I thought it would be this structured, professional system that would guide me from the clinical world I know into this new, mysterious world of motherhood. I expected that because I was part of the healthcare "in-crowd," I’d be even *better* cared for—that they would see me as a person who needed extra support because I was doing this on my own and because my professional brain was, frankly, a liability.

The most significant gap, reflecting on it all, is that I received *medical management* when I desperately needed *human support*. I was treated as a chart to be cleared, not a person to be held. They looked at my stats, they ticked the boxes, and they sent me on my way, completely ignoring the fact that I was a young, single mother struggling to reconcile my professional expertise with the raw, terrifying reality of loving a baby. 

They gave me a checklist for my body, but they left me entirely alone with my mind. I needed them to acknowledge that my background didn't make me invincible; it just made me more prone to hyper-vigilance. I needed them to see me—the mother—instead of just the colleague. That failure to see me has been the heaviest part of this entire journey.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

I would fundamentally change the way we assess "risk." Right now, the system is designed to triage patients based on physical markers—blood pressure, fetal heart rate, gestational age. If you’re a "low-risk" pregnancy, you’re essentially funneled through an assembly line of standard, abbreviated care. The system assumes that because you’re physically healthy, you’re mentally and emotionally equipped to handle the transition to motherhood without much intervention.

I want a system that acknowledges the *social* risk factors as part of the clinical profile. Being a single mother, or having a high-pressure career in medicine, shouldn't be invisible variables. They should trigger a higher level of psychological and emotional support. We need a system that recognizes that "wellness" isn’t just the absence of medical complications; it’s the presence of mental well-being.

I’d want a model where patients—especially those like me who might be perceived as "experts"—are routinely assigned a dedicated advocate who isn't there to check vitals or sign charts, but to check *in*. Someone who acknowledges that just because I know *how* to monitor a baby, doesn't mean I’m okay while doing it. We need to stop treating women like sets of symptoms and start treating them like the human beings they are.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the only thing I’d add is that I’m still waiting for someone to just stop the clock. I feel like I’ve been running a marathon in my own head since the moment I walked out of that hospital with her, and I’m still waiting for a handoff. 

People think that because I’m a nurse, I’m "equipped." They look at my life and think I have all the tools. But sometimes, being an expert feels like a cage. I’m tired of being the only one holding the weight. I’m tired of being the only one who has to be calm, the only one who has to be the medic, the only one who has to be the mom. 

I just want to be clear that even when a woman looks like she has it all together—when she knows the terminology, when she’s practiced and poised—she’s often just doing her best to hide the fact that she’s terrified. We need to stop assuming that competence in a medical setting translates to emotional stability at home. I’m a mother, not a shift-change report. I just really wish someone had asked me, *really* asked me, how I was doing—not just as a professional, but as a person—before it was too late. Thank you for asking. It’s the first time in a long time I’ve actually felt heard.
